related_content.title: Related Content
points: Epic of Gilgamesh - Hero's journey archetype

keyMyths.title: The Hero's Journey
keyMyths.description: A hero receives a call to adventure, faces trials, achieves a transformative victory, and returns with newfound wisdom or power to benefit their community. This narrative pattern appears across cultures and time periods, highlighting universal themes of growth and self-discovery.
keyMyths.source: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*

keyMyths.title: The Creation Myth
keyMyths.description: Explains the origins of the universe, the earth, and life, often involving divine beings or primordial forces. Creation myths provide a framework for understanding the world and humanity's place within it.

keyMyths.title: The Flood Myth
keyMyths.description: A catastrophic flood, often divinely sent, wipes out civilization, leaving only a few survivors who repopulate the earth. These stories explore themes of divine judgment, purification, and the cyclical nature of destruction and renewal.

keyMyths.title: The Descent to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: A character journeys to the realm of the dead, often to retrieve a loved one, seek knowledge, or confront their own mortality. The descent and return symbolize confronting the shadow self and achieving a deeper understanding of life and death.

keyMyths.title: The Trickster Tale
keyMyths.description: A mischievous figure, often an animal or deity, uses cunning and deception to disrupt the established order or challenge social norms. Tricksters represent chaos, creativity, and the potential for transformation.
keyMyths.source: Native American Trickster Tales (e.g., Coyote stories)

sources.name: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces* (1949)
sources.description: A seminal work analyzing the monomyth, or the common structure underlying various hero myths.
sources.title: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces* (1949)

sources.name: Carl Jung, *The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious* (1968)
sources.description: Jung's work provides the psychological framework for understanding archetypes as universal, inherited patterns of thought and behavior.
sources.title: Carl Jung, *The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious* (1968)

sources.name: Mircea Eliade, *Cosmos and History: The Myth of the Eternal Return* (1954)
sources.description: Explores the cyclical nature of myth and the human desire to return to a sacred, primordial time.
sources.title: Mircea Eliade, *Cosmos and History: The Myth of the Eternal Return* (1954)

sources.name: Claude Lévi-Strauss, *Structural Anthropology* (1958)
sources.description: Lévi-Strauss's structuralist approach analyzes myths as systems of binary oppositions, revealing underlying structures of human thought.
sources.title: Claude Lévi-Strauss, *Structural Anthropology* (1958)

sources.name: Stith Thompson, *Motif-Index of Folk-Literature* (1955-1958)
sources.description: A comprehensive index of recurring narrative elements (motifs) found in folklore and mythology worldwide, serving as a valuable tool for comparative analysis.
sources.title: Stith Thompson, *Motif-Index of Folk-Literature* (1955-1958)
